coredata.dc:title: Efficient detection and degradation of hexavalent chromium wastewater in tourist attractions using dual-fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 stabilized polysulfone Pickering emulsions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Zhao, Rui
Yu, Dehai
coredata.dc:description: This investigation delineates the synthesis of a novel, bifunctional composite material, embedding cadmium telluride (CdTe) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within the metal-organic framework UiO-66-NH2, fabricated through a Pickering emulsion technique to augment the photocatalytic degradation and detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). We elucidate that the integration of these quantum dots substantially narrows the bandgap energy of UiO-66-NH2 to 1.76 eV for CQDs and 2.83 eV for the composite, thereby enhancing the spectrum of visible light absorption and fortifying charge carrier separation. The foundlings reveal a pronounced elevation in photocatalytic performance, with the composite material accomplishing up to 90 % reduction of Cr(VI) within 200 min under visible light exposure. Moreover, the composite demonstrates robust reusability, sustaining efficacious degradation across numerous cycles with minimal attenuation in activity. The composite material can detect and degrade Cr(VI) in the environment, providing an efficient means of heavy metal pollution remediation for tourist attractions. It is economical and sustainable, aligning with the green and low-carbon tourism development concept.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pickering emulsion
Dual-fluorescent
Metal-organic frameworks
Quantum dots
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dual fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles (DFUs)
Preparation and characterization of dual fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 stabilized Polysulfone Pickering emulsion (DFUP)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020MB128).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of DFUs. (b) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, CdTeQDs, UiO-66-NH2, and DFUs. (c) Fluorescence emission wavelengths of DFU-1.0 under different excitation wavelengths. (d) XPS spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and DFU-1.0. Corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra for O 1 s (e) and Zr 3d (f). (g) The relative ratios of fluorescence intensity (I629/I455) of DFUs under different metal ions (5 mg/L).

(a) Pickering emulsion stabilized by DFUs. (b) SEM images of porous microspheres of UiO-66-NH2/PSF and DFUP-x with different CQDs additions, and (c) DFUs covering the oil-water interface. (d) SEM image of the hollow microsphere of DFUP-1.0. (e, f) CLSM images of porous microspheres of DFUP-x at excitation wavelengths of 455 nm and 629 nm respectively. The embedded images are corresponding microscope photos under visible light.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) with UiO-66-NH2/PSF and DFUP-x. (b) Catalytic degradation kinetics rate constants for UiO-66-NH2/PSF and DFUP-x on Cr(VI). (c) Photocatalytic degradation curves of DFUP-x over five cycles; (d) Recyclability of the DFUP-1.0 sample under different cycle numbers, with an inset showing the adsorption and degradation efficiency for Cr(VI) solution after recycling the DFUP-1.0 sample five times. The insert SEM images are the corresponding DFUP-1.0.

(a) Tauc plot of CQD, UiO-66-NH2, and CQD@UiO-66-NH2. (b) Transient photocurrent response in DFUP-x under visible light irradiation; (c) Nyquist plot; (e) DMPO-·OH ESR spectrum; (f) DMPO-·O2- ESR spectrum. Mechanism diagram of photocatalytic oxidation-reduction degradation of Cr(VI) with DFUP microspheres.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of DFUs and its application in the degradation and detection of Cr(VI).
Comparison of the photocatalysts and their photocatalytic performance of pollutant degradation.

head.ce:title: Efficient detection and degradation of hexavalent chromium wastewater in tourist attractions using dual-fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 stabilized polysulfone Pickering emulsions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Tourism, Shandong Women's University, Jinan, Shandong 250300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Tourism, Shandong Women's University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Tourism, Shandong Women's University, Jinan, Shandong, 250300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology, Shandong Academy of Sciences, Ji’nan, Shandong Province 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology, Shandong Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology, Shandong Academy of Sciences, Ji’nan, Shandong Province 250353, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This investigation delineates the synthesis of a novel, bifunctional composite material, embedding cadmium telluride (CdTe) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within the metal-organic framework UiO-66-NH2, fabricated through a Pickering emulsion technique to augment the photocatalytic degradation and detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). We elucidate that the integration of these quantum dots substantially narrows the bandgap energy of UiO-66-NH2 to 1.76 eV for CQDs and 2.83 eV for the composite, thereby enhancing the spectrum of visible light absorption and fortifying charge carrier separation. The foundlings reveal a pronounced elevation in photocatalytic performance, with the composite material accomplishing up to 90 % reduction of Cr(VI) within 200 min under visible light exposure. Moreover, the composite demonstrates robust reusability, sustaining efficacious degradation across numerous cycles with minimal attenuation in activity. The composite material can detect and degrade Cr(VI) in the environment, providing an efficient means of heavy metal pollution remediation for tourist attractions. It is economical and sustainable, aligning with the green and low-carbon tourism development concept.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tourism, as an important pillar of global economic development, has received increasing attention in recent years. However, the rapid development of tourism has also brought a series of problems such as environmental pollution, especially water pollution caused by industrial wastewater discharge, which has negatively impacted the environmental quality of tourist destinations and the experience of visitors [1]. In certain tourist destinations, maintenance and cleaning of facilities may involve the use of chromium-containing chemicals, such as metal surface cleaners, anti-corrosion coatings, or specialized cleaning agents for concrete and stone surfaces [2–4]. These chemicals, particularly those containing hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), can contribute to environmental contamination if not properly managed [5,6]. Although the use of such chemicals has diminished over time, there is still a potential risk of chromium pollution in areas where they are utilized for protective or cleaning purposes. Hexavalent chromium is a heavy metal pollutant widely present in industrial wastewater. It is highly toxic and easily absorbed by organisms, posing a serious threat to the ecological environment and public health in tourist areas [7,8]. Given its profound toxicity and carcinogenic potential, hexavalent chromium represents a critical environmental concern [9]. Consequently, the development of efficacious and precise detection and degradation methodologies for Cr(VI) is imperative to mitigate its environmental impact. Traditional detection approaches, while sensitive and accurate, typically necessitate intricate procedures and rely on costly equipment such as Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), Ion Chromatography (IC), and Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10–12]. Conversely, conventional degradation techniques are often hampered by elevated operational costs and stringent conditions, which curtail their practical deployment. In the realm of contemporary research, the proliferation of nanomaterials has ushered in innovative chemical sensors for various pollutants, including novel modalities for Cr(VI) detection [13,14]. Despite their remarkable sensitivity, these sensors are frequently constrained by laborious fabrication processes [15] and limited selectivity [16]. The ongoing advancement of nanotechnology, however, presents novel avenues for addressing these environmental quandaries. Quantum dots, as emergent nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention owing to their distinctive size-dependent properties and superior optical characteristics. Notably, cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTeQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exemplify this class with their exceptional optical prowess and chemical resilience [17,18], promising extensive utility in environmental monitoring and remediation. Nonetheless, the direct application of quantum dots in environmental contexts is often impeded by issues like stability and dispersibility. To circumvent these challenges, there is growing interest in amalgamating quantum dots with other functional materials to fabricate composite materials that enhance their environmental applicability. Among potential candidates, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), with their expansive surface areas, adjustable pore configurations, and versatile physicochemical attributes, stand out [19]. Particularly, UiO-66-NH2 is distinguished by its large pore sizes, substantial surface area, and abundant functional groups, making it an ideal candidate for diverse applications spanning adsorption to catalysis [20,21].

The synthesis of porous materials with intricate structures has gained prominence across diverse sectors, notably water treatment and catalysis, where the efficacy of such structures can be transformative. An efficacious strategy for crafting these architectures is the application of the Pickering emulsion template method [22,23]. This technique has been pivotal in developing porous adsorbents with enhanced adsorption efficiencies [22] and sustained release functionalities [24–26]. Furthermore, it has facilitated the in-situ synthesis of hybrid microspheres combining cellulose nanocrystals with zeolitic imidazolate frameworks, underscoring the adaptability of this method [27]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), renowned for their distinct porous structures and adjustable properties, have been extensively explored for applications in catalysis and gas adsorption [28]. The strategic design and synthesis of MOF-derived materials featuring hierarchical porosity have markedly bolstered their catalytic efficiency [28]. For instance, porous cobalt disulfide nanoparticles, produced via gaseous sulfurization of MOFs, have emerged as bifunctional electrocatalysts for comprehensive water splitting, exemplifying the utility of MOF-based constructs in energy applications [29]. Moreover, integrating Pickering emulsion templating with MOFs has generated innovative materials possessing unique characteristics [5,30,31]. A notable development is the creation of MOF-based microcapsules, assembled via self-organization of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 nanoparticles at oil-water interfaces, which are tailored for size-selective interfacial enzymatic catalysis [32,33]. Additionally, hierarchically porous ZIF-8 materials have been synthesized as Pickering interfacial catalysts, demonstrating the versatility of MOFs in catalytic roles [34–36]. The combination of Pickering emulsion templating and metal-organic frameworks has shown great promise in the design and fabrication of porous materials with enhanced properties for various applications [37]. Despite the vast potential, reports on employing this composite strategy as a carrier for nanoprobes or in novel preparatory techniques remain sparse.

This work introduces the encapsulation of CdTeQDs and CQDs dual quantum dots within the UiO-66-NH2 framework, utilizing solvent evaporation to affix these dual-fluorescent MOF composite nanoparticles onto the surface of porous and hollow polysulfone microspheres via the Pickering emulsion template method ( Scheme 1). This dual-functional material facilitates visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of hexavalent chromium and supports continuous visual recognition under UV light. The CQDs enhance the generation of electron-hole pairs across the microsphere surface, optimizing the light utilization rate for effective photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI). Concurrently, CdTeQDs provide a robust and facile detection mechanism for hexavalent chromium. This composite not only integrates the optical attributes of CdTeQDs and CQDs within a MOF scaffold but also leverages the structural advantages and reusability of polysulfone hollow microspheres, thus offering innovative approaches for both the detection and degradation of Cr(VI).

The reagents used in this study were citric acid, polysulfone (PSF), dichloromethane (DCM, ≥99.5 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), ethylenediamine, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), methylene blue, zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 97 %), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, ≥ 99.5 %), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, 98 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 98 %), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), all obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The wastewater sample containing hexavalent chromium was collected near a tourist attraction in Jinan City (latitude 36.6512°N, longitude 117.1201°E), The solution was prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm, obtained from FLEX4, ElgaPURELAB, UK. The previously specified reagents were of high quality and did not need any further purification procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles (DFUs)
ce:section.ce:para: Solvothermal methods produced UiO-66-NH2 [38,39]. In 50 mL of DMF, 0.65 g of ZrCl4 and 0.51 g of NH2-BDC were homogenized for 15 min by ultrasonic agitation. After adding 10 mL of acetic acid, the mixture was agitated for two hours. The mixture was then placed in a high-pressure autoclave at 130°C for a complete diurnal cycle. The post-reaction yield was washed with DMF and anhydrous ethanol sequentially and dried at 100°C for 12 h. In another CQD synthesis method, 5 g citric acid and 2.5 g ethylenediamine were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water in a round-bottom flask. This solution was stirred at 160°C for 6 h. Following cooling, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1 k was used to purify the solution in deionized water for 48 h with water changes every two hours. The purified solution was freeze-dried to make amine-functionalized CQDs. Several improvements were made to literature-based CdTe quantum dot synthesis [40]. First, 40.0 mL of a 0.02 mol/L CdCl2 solution was added to a three-necked flask and pH adjusted to 11.5 with 2.0 mol/L sodium hydroxide. The solution was nitrogen-purged for 20 min. A certain amount of NaHTe was quickly dissolved and added into the violently agitated solution. The 1:2:0.25 Cd2+:TGA:Te- stoichiometric ratio was carefully maintained. The CdTe precursor was heated at 140°C for a set time in a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave to create fluorescent quantum dots. These quantum dots were purified by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 5 minutes in methanol thrice the sample volume to remove unreacted precursors like Cd+. After vacuum drying, the precipitate was redissolved in 40 mL of distilled water and stored at 4°C. In the next step, dual fluorescent materials were added to ZIF-8 to make the CCU. The combination of 2-methylimidazole (16.0 mmol), 1.7 mL of InPQDs (50 nmol/L), 120 μg of CDs, and 30 mL of methanol was agitated for one hour. After adding Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O (1.0 mmol) to the mixture, it was agitated for eight hours and left to stand for two hours. The product was centrifuged and vacuum-dried at 50°C. The last step was adding 0.2 wt% CQD and 0.1 wt% CdTeQD to a 1 wt% UiO-66-NH2 aqueous solution. To aid bonding, the mixture was magnetically agitated for 24 h with EDC/NHS (0.1 and 0.3 wt% to UiO-66-NH2). Multiple 1000-rpm centrifugations with deionized water eliminated excess reactants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of dual fluorescent UiO-66-NH2 stabilized Polysulfone Pickering emulsion (DFUP)
ce:section.ce:para:
DFUP Pickering microspheres were meticulously made via Pickering emulsion evaporation. Dissolving 0.125 g PSF and 0.1 g PVP in 6.0 milliliters of a 1:5 NMP-DCM solvent produced a clear solution. CCUs with 0.2–1.0 wt% CQDs were introduced to this solution at 0.11 g. Stirring the solution for 30 minutes dissolved all components. This solution was carefully put into a coagulation bath with 50 mL of 1 % PVA via microchannels to create DFUP hollow microspheres. The system was agitated at room temperature for five hours to develop properly. After stirring, the microspheres were collected using a 500-mesh sieve, rinsed with ethanol and distilled water, then filtered to remove loose DFUs. Drying at 60°C yielded DFUP-x, where 'x' denotes the weight ratio of CQDs to UiO-66-NH2, which ranged from 0.2 to 1.0. PSF microspheres without DFUs and those with UiO-66-NH2 were also made using the same procedure. Advanced methods were used to analyze microsphere structure and composition. Their surface morphology was studied using a Hitachi Regulus 8220 SEM. DFUP-x microsphere fluorescence was assessed using a Leica SP8-X confocal laser scanning microscope. To prepare for this assessment, 5 mL of microspheres were dispersed in 8 mL of water and a 1 mL sample was put on a microscope slide with a glass coverslip. The DFUP-x microspheres had two excitation peaks at 455 and 629 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy examined microsphere surface elements. A Bruker D8Advance X-ray diffractometer collected diffraction patterns from 5° to 90° to study the crystalline structure. FT-IR using a Bruker Vertex-V70 instrument identified DFUP functional groupings. These analyses were repeated three times per sample and averaged for accuracy. Porosity and density of the microspheres were measured using traditional gravimetric methods using an equation. Three tests were performed on each sample to ensure repeatability. Porosity was determined gravimetrically using a formula:
(1)
P
%
=
W
A
−
W
B
ρ
W
W
B
ρ
P
+
W
B
−
W
A
ρ
W
× 100

The formula for density calculation is as follows (2):
(2)
D =
1 − P
W
M
ρ
M
+
W
P
ρ
P
where WB and WA are the microsphere weights before and after drying, and WM and WP are the composite microsphere's DFU and PSF masses. The densities of water, PSF, and UiO-66-NH2 are ρW (1.0 g/cm³), ρP (1.24 g/cm³), and ρM (0.82 g/cm³). XPS (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to investigate CCU chemical composition. The optical properties of CCUP were evaluated using a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV/Vis spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F4600 fluorescence spectrometer. DFUP photoelectrochemical characteristics were assessed using a Chinese CS310 electrochemical workstation. To conduct active substance capture studies and ESR analysis, TBA was employed to scavenge ·OH radicals, while EDTA and BQ were used to scavenge ·O2 -. The effect of these active compounds on MB removal by CUP-x was examined using ·O2 - scavenging. Bruker EMX Plus ESR spectra were acquired under simulated sunshine conditions to identify ·OH and ·O2- generated by DFUP-x/H2O2 using spin traps containing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). To detect TEMPN DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH production, dissolve 10 mg DFUP in 0.5 mL deionized water with 45 μL DMPO in simulated sunshine. DFUP-x microsphere structure was carefully studied using a Micromeritics 9620 mercury intrusion porosimeter. For this technique, the sample must be heated in an oven at 105°C for at least 24 hours to remove all moisture and water vapor. Mercury intrusion porosimetry uses 0.7 g, or 0.3 cm3 samples. The porosimeter can detect 5 nm pore diameters at 414 MPa.

Distribute 20 mg of DFUP evenly in 10 mL of HEPES buffer solution with a concentration of 0.06 mol/L and a pH of 8.0. Apply ultrasonic waves to the mixture for a duration of 2 min. To investigate the relationship between DFUs and Cr(VI), combine 100 μL of a 2 mg/mL solution of DFU with 1.5 mL of the same HEPES buffer. Next, add several amounts (ranging from 0 to 10 mg/L) of Cr(VI) to the mixture. Dilute the whole amount to 2 mL by adding more HEPES buffer. After agitating the solution at the ambient temperature for a duration of 3 min, proceed to stimulate it at a wavelength of 360 nm and document the resulting emission spectra. Obtain the fluorescence emission intensities at wavelengths of 629 nm and 455 nm in order to examine their respective ratios. The minimum detection concentration of the DFUP composite microsphere system for detecting inorganic pollutants is 97.88 μg/L.

In order to determine how well DFUP, UiO-66-NH2, and PSF degrade Cr(VI) by photocatalysis, we used the XPA-7 equipment, which was provided by Nanjing Xujiang Electromechanical Plant. The trials were place in natural settings. The light needed for the reduction of Cr(VI) was supplied by a 350 W xenon lamp, which was 8 cm away from a reflecting surface. The apparatus had a UV filter that eliminated light with a wavelength less than 420 nm. The first step was to dissolve 30 mg of the chosen photocatalyst in 40 mL of a 20 mg/L Cr(VI) solution. Attaining adsorption equilibrium required stirring the liquid in the dark for thirty minutes. To start the photocatalytic process, the solution was then exposed to ultraviolet light from the xenon lamp. A 1.0 mL sample was taken from the mixture every 15 min. Afterwards, a centrifuge was used to spin down these materials. A UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the concentration of Cr(VI) that remained after each interval. An equation referred to as formula (3) was used to calculate the rate of Cr(VI) degradation:
(3)
Degradation = ( C −
C
0
) /
C
r 0

where, C
0 and C represent the concentrations of the dye at the start of irradiation (t=0) and at a given time t, respectively. DFUP-x microspheres, containing CQD levels of 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1.0 wt% relative to UiO-66-NH2, were utilized as photocatalysts in a 40 mL aqueous Cr(VI) solution (20 mg/L), under simulated sunlight conditions for the photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI). The light source was a 350 W xenon lamp fitted with an infrared cutoff filter (λ>800 nm). Prior to exposure to light, the solution was stirred in darkness for 30 minutes to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and Cr(VI). Samples from the treated solution were collected every 15 min and the concentration of Cr(VI) was measured at 560 nm using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. Following the photocatalytic reaction, DFUP microspheres were separated by centrifugation, thoroughly rinsed with deionized water to eliminate any remaining impurities, and dried for reuse in further experiments.

The dual fluorescent UiO-66-NH2-polysulfone (DFUP) composite microspheres, as delineated in Scheme 1, have been engineered to fulfill dual functionalities: the photocatalytic degradation of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and its real-time fluorescence detection. These microspheres incorporate a blend of CQDs, CdTeQDs, and UiO-66-NH2, mixed within a solvent matrix of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and dichloromethane (DCM). Through the employment of a Pickering emulsion template method, this precursor mixture yields porous, hollow microspheres stabilized by polysulfone. Functionally, these DFUP microspheres exhibit dual capabilities. They actively catalyze the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) under visible light at the oil/water (O/W) interface, a transformation evident by the color change in the solution. Concurrently, the embedded quantum dots, possessing intrinsic fluorescent properties, enable the real-time detection of Cr(VI) via fluorescence quenching upon exposure to the pollutant. Observations from subsequent insets reveal significant changes in solution color and fluorescence intensity before and after exposure to Cr(VI), confirming the efficacy of DFUP microspheres in environmental remediation and monitoring.

Fig. 1a presents a TEM image of the DFUP composite particles, highlighting their crystalline structure and distinct irregular polyhedral shape. The homogeneous distribution of CQDs and CdTeQDs on the UiO-66-NH2 matrix, as depicted in the enlarged inset, not only evidences a high level of surface compatibility but also suggests potential synergistic interactions between the quantum dots and the MOF scaffold. This strategic incorporation of CQDs and CdTeQDs is pivotal, likely enhancing the composite's physicochemical properties and extending its utility beyond photocatalysis to applications in optoelectronics.

Fig. 1b elucidates the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2, CQDs, CdTeQDs, and DFUs, which affirm the integration of these components within the composite. The spectrum of CQDs reveals N-H bending and C-H stretching vibration peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1, respectively. A marked attenuation of the latter in the DFU spectrum suggests a covalent linkage of CQDs to the UiO-66-NH2 framework, accompanied by a reduction in free C-H groups. The introduction of CdTeQDs into the composite maintains the characteristic spectral peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1389 cm−1, indicating the preservation of their functional groups. The UiO-66-NH2 spectrum displays a broad band at 3449 cm−1 from O-H stretching and a distinct peak at 1657 cm−1 associated with N-H bending in amine groups, suggestive of hydroxyl groups or adsorbed water molecules. These spectral features in the DFU composite underscore its structural integrity and successful integration of quantum dots into the MOF matrix, enhancing its functionality for both photocatalytic degradation and fluorescence-based environmental monitoring.

Fig. 1c presents the fluorescence emission spectra of DFU-1.0, showing a robust visible range emission peak that underscores the sustained fluorescence response of the composite over time. The slight shift and intensity variations of the emission peak with changing excitation wavelengths highlight the adaptable photophysical properties of DFU-1.0. Additionally, the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra detailed in Fig. 1d illustrate the surface chemistry of both DFU-1.0 and UiO-66-NH2. The survey spectrum reveals the elemental composition, evidenced by the presence of peaks corresponding to O 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, Zr 3d, Cd 3d, and Te 3d. Notably, the emergence of Cd and Te peaks in the DFU-1.0 spectrum confirms the effective integration of CdTe quantum dots. High-resolution O 1 s spectra of DFU-1.0 and UiO-66-NH2 exhibit peaks related to adsorbed and lattice oxygen, with slight modifications in the DFU-1.0 spectrum indicating alterations in the oxygen environment due to the incorporation of quantum dots [41]. Additionally, the Zr 3d spectra display doublet peaks at approximately 182.4 eV and 184.7 eV, corresponding to Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2, respectively [42], confirming the preservation of the MOF's fundamental structure within the composite. This comprehensive XPS analysis not only validates the effective compositional integration but also the subtle changes in the electronic environment, crucial for the DFU-1.0 composite's designated photocatalytic and sensing functionalities.

Fig. 1g showcases the specificity of the DFUs for selectively detecting hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) amidst a milieu of diverse metal ions, each at a concentration of 5 mg/L. Notably, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I629/I455) of DFUs significantly decreases in the presence of Cr(VI), indicating pronounced quenching, a response markedly distinct from other tested cations. While iron (III) ions exhibit moderate quenching, all other cations, including potassium, sodium, calcium, magnesium, iron (II), aluminum, zinc, tin, lead, silver, copper, barium, and lithium, show negligible effects on the fluorescence emission. This exceptional selectivity underscores the DFUs' efficacy as a dual fluorescent quantum dot-based probe, offering high accuracy and specificity for Cr(VI) detection, making it an excellent candidate for monitoring this toxic metal ion in environmental contexts.

Fig. 2 illustrates a complete examination of DFUP microspheres using SEM, which gives substantial information about the various structures and compositions of these microspheres. A Pickering emulsion is shown in panel (a), which demonstrates that DFUP microspheres are able to efficiently stabilize the oil-water interface. This demonstrates that the microspheres are providing effective stabilization. Panels (b1) through (b5) illustrate the morphology of the control microspheres as well as the microspheres that have been improved with DFUP. There is a discernible increase in porosity and roughness on the surface as the concentration of quantum dots increases from DFUP-0.2 to DFUP-1.0. This indicates that there is an increase in surface area and maybe a greater level of reactivity of the microspheres. In panel (c), a comprehensive image of the oil-water interface is shown, with a particular emphasis on a DFUP-1.0 microsphere that exhibits a thick coating of DFUs. In order for the microsphere to exert its sensing and catalytic capabilities, this extensive covering is absolutely necessary. Panel (d) investigates the hollow structure of the DFUP-1.0 microsphere in order to highlight its uniform wall thickness. This is an essential function that must be fulfilled in order to guarantee the microsphere's stability and effectiveness in application. As a result of their lower density, microspheres with this hollow structure are able to demonstrate buoyancy in aquatic conditions, which enables them to be recycled and reused several times.

In Fig. 2e and f, the fluorescence characteristics of DFUP microspheres were visually investigated using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) at 455 nm and 629 nm, respectively, for the purpose of excitation. There is a direct correlation between the concentration of DFUs and the fluorescence intensities of microspheres. This illustrates that the dots are able to effectively improve fluorescence while maintaining the microspheres' apparent appearance under standard lighting conditions, as are illustrated in the visual insets. According to the findings, the DFUP microspheres serve a twofold purpose: first, they provide the capability of varying fluorescence, and second, they manage to maintain a consistent distribution of quantum dots, which allows for even fluorescence. These properties are essential for determining the levels of pollution and removing contaminants such as hexavalent chromium via the process of photocatalysis. Both the optical and catalytic capabilities of the microspheres, in addition to their one-of-a-kind mix of features, making them an excellent choice for the targeted detection and removal of pollutants in water.

The process of degradation Cr(VI) using DFUP microspheres is illustrated in Fig. 3a. It illustrates the temporal evolution of Cr(VI) concentration, where C0 denotes the initial concentration and C signifies the concentration at subsequent time intervals. The degradation profiles clearly indicate that DFUP-1.0, which contains the highest concentration of QDs, exhibits a significantly accelerated degradation rate in comparison to the control sample of UiO-66-NH2/PSF and other DFUP variants with lower QD contents. A clear correlation has been observed between higher concentrations of QD and improved photocatalytic activity, as indicated by the observed pattern. The utilization of QDs is expected to result in enhanced surface area and improved electron and hole separation. The graph in Fig. 3b illustrates the logarithmic conversion of the concentration ratios, denoted as ln(C/C0), plotted against time. This plot is utilized to analyze the kinetics of the reaction using a first-order model. The results indicate a clear correlation between the rate constants and the concentration of QDs. The DFUP-1.0 microspheres exhibit a notable enhancement, suggesting a greater rate constant and consequently improved photocatalytic efficacy of this formulation. The observed enhancement can be attributed to the capacity of QDs to expand light absorption into the visible spectrum. This results in an augmented quantity of electron-hole pairs, thereby facilitating more efficient charge separation. These characteristics greatly decrease the probability of electron-hole recombination, allowing for the maintenance of outstanding photocatalytic performance for prolonged durations.

Fig. 3c presents the photocatalytic degradation curves of DFUP-x microspheres over five consecutive cycles, illustrating the degradation of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). The graph shows the normalized concentration (C/C0) of Cr(VI) throughout each cycle, where C 0 is the initial concentration and C is the concentration at various time points. Notably, the degradation curves from the first to the fifth cycle are closely superimposed, indicating that the photocatalytic activity of the microspheres is maintained across multiple uses without significant loss of efficiency. This consistent performance across cycles suggests that the DFUP-x microspheres possess exceptional stability and reusability, with no observable deactivation even after repeated use. The rapid decrease in Cr(VI) concentration within the first 40 minutes and the near-complete degradation by 200 minutes in each cycle underscore the high efficiency and robustness of the photocatalyst. The sustained catalytic activity can be attributed to the durable encapsulation of carbon quantum dots within the microsphere matrix, which likely protects the active sites and preserves the integrity of the metal-organic framework under photocatalytic conditions. Photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) after five consecutive cycles shows that DFUP-1.0 microspheres may be recycled and remain durable (Fig. 3d). The graph shows that there is no discernible drop in activity, with rates of photocatalytic degradation being consistently high from the first to the fifth cycle. The photocatalyst's robustness and longevity are shown here. On the other hand, dark absorption rates are much lower, indicating that light is the primary driver of degradation. The microspheres show no signs of physical deterioration in scanning electron microscope (SEM) images taken before and after the cycles. There are a variety of environmental uses for DFUP-1.0 microspheres, including solar-powered processes for cleaning water that is polluted with Cr(VI), due to their long-lasting photocatalytic efficacy and structural stability. Here we have an eco-friendly and effective method for treating water that will endure.

In Fig. 4a, the Tauc plots for CQDs, UiO-66-NH2, and CQD@UiO-66-NH2 are shown. A significant amount of information on the electrical characteristics of the chemicals in issue may be obtained from these plots. For the purpose of enhancing their ability to absorb visible light, the CQDs have a bandgap that is just 1.76 electron volts, which is the minimal value. Due to the fact that pure UiO-66-NH2 has a bandgap magnitude of 2.71 electron volts, its absorption is restricted to the ultraviolet (UV) region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Through the inclusion of CQDs into UiO-66-NH2, a composite material is produced. This material also has a bandgap that is lowered to 2.83 eV as a consequence of the incorporation of CQDs. This leads in a significant enhancement in its capability to have the ability to absorb visible light when compared to in comparison to the pure MOF [43]. The decrease in bandgap, which indicates that there has been a change in the electronic structure of UiO-66-NH2, may have been caused by the presence of electrically active states that were produced by CQDs or by modifications at the band edges.

The brief variations in photocurrent that are seen in DFUP-x composites when they are exposed to visible light are shown in Fig. 4b. In the process of increasing the concentration of CQDs from 0.2 wt percent to 1.0 wt percent, it demonstrates a notable increase in photocurrent. This trend indicates that there has been an improvement in the production of charge carriers and a drop in the rates of recombination, which is particularly visible in the DFUP-1.0 composite [44]. In Fig. 4c, the electrochemical impedance spectra, also known as EIS, are shown. The DFUP series has the shallowest semicircle radius of all the series, which indicates that it has the least amount of interfacial charge transfer resistance. Improvements in electron mobility and transfer efficiency are needed for effective photocatalysis, and these properties imply that these improvements have occurred [45]. These results show that adding CQD to DFUP composites increases their photoactivity by a statistically significant increase in their ability to produce and move electrons. This indicates that these composites may be very successful photocatalysts for the removal of Cr(VI) in water treatment applications.

Fig. 4d and e provide the results of electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy investigations of reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation in DFUP-x composites during photocatalytic processes. DMPO acts here as a spin trap. Photocatalysis produces extremely reactive oxygen species (ROS) called hydroxyl radicals (·OH). One can see the hydroxyl radicals being produced in Fig. 4d. As the ESR signal magnitudes vary from 0.2–1.0, so does the weight percentage of CQD in the composites. Higher ·OH radical synthesis of the DFUP-1.0 composite is indicated by its greatest signal strength. The relationship discovered suggests that, particularly in the ·OH radical synthesis, photocatalytic activity rises dramatically with increasing CQD concentration. Better generation of ROS may be attributed to the increased surface area and charge carrier separation caused by the greater concentration of colloidal quantum dots (CQD). The DMPO-·O2 - spectrum measures the synthesis of superoxide radicals (·O2 -), and is similar in purpose to the DMPO-·OH spectrum. Once more shown is the significant increase in radical synthesis that happens at high CQD values. The observed effects of hydroxyl radicals are matched with the highest prominence of the ESR signals of DFUP-1.0. This phenomenon highlights the importance of CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic generation of ROS, which are essential for the effective degradation of contaminants.

Photocatalytic oxidation-reduction degradation of Cr(VI) by DFUP microspheres is shown in Fig. 4f by the intricate interplay of band alignments between UiO-66-NH2, CQDs, and CdTe. This arrangement has been meticulously designed to optimize the charge transfer processes that are crucial to photocatalysis. Particularly important for electron transport is UiO-66-NH2, which has a VB potential of 2.83 eV and a CB potential of −0.50 eV. A path for the efficient reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is mapped out by CQDs with VB potential of 1.74 eV and CB potential of −0.53 eV, which effectively bridge the electron transport to CdTe's CB at −0.74 eV. In addition, CQDs reduce electron-hole recombination and increase electron availability for the reduction process by improving visible light absorption and enabling electron migration across materials from the VB to the CB. As hydroxyl radicals are formed by the reaction of water with holes in the VB of UiO-66-NH2, which are necessary for oxidative degradation processes, electrons are transferred from the VB to the CB of CQDs and finally to the CB of CdTe when visible light is shone on them. Both the light absorption and the efficient separation and transportation of charge carriers are improved by the cooperative influence of the composite components and the strategic arrangement of energy levels. This arrangement ensures that the photosynthesis-generated electrons and holes are used effectively in redox reactions, leading to a significant increase in photocatalytic efficiency. In addition to stabilizing the charge carriers and reducing recombination rates, the contact between the composite materials also provides a continuous channel for Cr(VI) degradation.

Table 1 systematically compares the performance of various advanced photocatalysts in the degradation of Cr(VI) in aqueous solutions, with a particular focus on the pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, light source used, and reduction efficiency over a specified time. Notably, the novel DFPC catalyst demonstrated in this study exhibited an outstanding reduction efficiency of 99 % for a 20 mg/L Cr(VI) solution in the presence of a 350 W xenon lamp for 140 min. This exceeds the benchmark set by established photocatalysts such as NNU-36 MOF, various carbonitrides, and bismuth tungstate composites, which have efficiencies ranging from 66 % to 98 % under similar conditions [46–51]. The superior efficacy of DFPC, as evidenced by the accelerated degradation rate and higher efficiency, highlights its potential as a primary material for the photoremediation of heavy metal ion-contaminated water systems. The DFUP microspheres have a solid track record of effectively treating contaminated water sources, positioning them as a sustainable and environmentally friendly option for addressing industrial pollution.

This study successfully demonstrated the use of CdTe and CQDs quantum dot-integrated UiO-66-NH2 microspheres for the photocatalytic degradation and detection of Cr(VI) via the application of a Pickering emulsion approach. The used methodology was very effective in facilitating the uniform integration of quantum dots into the MOF framework, hence enhancing the photocatalytic properties of the composites. The composites that were created had a remarkable ability to absorb visible light for a longer duration and achieve improved separation of electrons and holes. This was achieved by significantly reducing the bandgap, which reached as low as 1.76 eV for CQDs and 2.83 eV for the composite. The experimental results showed that Cr(VI) degradation reached up to 90 % after 200 min of exposure to visible light. Additionally, the material demonstrated excellent reusability, maintaining its efficiency throughout several cycles without any loss. The findings highlight the capability of the DFUP-x microspheres as very effective photocatalysts for environmental remediation, particularly in removing hazardous Cr(VI) from water. The composite material can not only effectively detect the concentration of Cr(VI) in the environment but also achieve its efficient degradation using visible light, providing a feasible means of heavy metal pollution remediation for tourist attractions and their surrounding areas, thus supporting the sustainable development of the tourism industry. Moreover, the high reusability of this material also means it has economic feasibility and lasting application value in the environmental remediation process, aligning with the green and low-carbon tourism development concept.

Dehai Yu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Rui Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Ying Liu: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020MB128).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The interplay of sulfur doping and surface hydroxyl in band gap engineering: mesoporous sulfur-doped TiO2 coupled with magnetite as a recyclable, efficient, visible light active photocatalyst for water purification
Appl. Catal. B: Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yan, K. Yuan, N. Lu, H. Xu, S. Zhang, N. Takeuchi, H. Kobayashi, R. Li, The interplay of sulfur doping and surface hydroxyl in band gap engineering: mesoporous sulfur-doped TiO2 coupled with magnetite as a recyclable, efficient, visible light active photocatalyst for water purification, Appl. Catal. B: Environ., 218 (2017) 20-31.
